nodes.title: être — to be

explanation.body: Être covers what someone is, how something is, and where things are. But French draws the line differently than English: age, hunger, thirst, heat, and cold all belong to avoir. Translating 'I am hungry' word for word is the number-one être mistake.

examples.french: Je suis fatigué. On est au café.
examples.english: I'm tired. We're at the café.

examples.french: J'ai faim. J'ai trente ans.
examples.english: I'm hungry. I'm thirty. (avoir, not être)

explanation.body: Suis, es, est, sont do nearly all the spoken work. You already use est constantly — c'est is just ce + est, so every c'est sentence you've built was être practice. And on est handles 'we are'; nous sommes doesn't get said.

examples.french: C'est bon. Elle est là. Ils sont où ?
examples.english: It's good. She's here. Where are they?

explanation.body: Je suis compresses to j'suis, often down to one syllable — chuis. Tu es is nearly always t'es.

examples.french: Chuis là. T'es prêt ?
examples.english: I'm here. You ready?

explanation.body: Reach for the imparfait first: states and descriptions are background, and c'était is how you review anything — a meal, a film, a weekend. The passé composé j'ai été exists, but mostly for one-off events.

examples.french: C'était super. T'étais où ?
examples.english: It was great. Where were you?

nodes.street_notes: je suis → j'suis, often chuis in fast speech; tu es → t'es (near-universal); c'est carries most of the load in real speech.

nodes.title: avoir — to have

explanation.body: Avoir means 'to have', but French assigns several states to it that English puts under 'be': age, hunger, thirst, fear, luck, and need. Translating those with être is the most common English-speaker mistake.

examples.french: J'ai vingt ans. T'as faim ? Il a besoin de rien.
examples.english: I'm twenty. Are you hungry? He doesn't need anything.

explanation.header: il y a — the one fixed phrase
explanation.body: Il y a means 'there is / there are' and, with a time expression, 'ago'. In speech it collapses to y'a almost every time. Learn it as a fixed chunk — y'a pas means 'there isn't any' and y'a rien means 'there's nothing'.

examples.french: Y'a du monde. Y'a deux jours.
examples.english: There's a crowd. Two days ago.

explanation.header: Street forms: what actually gets said
explanation.body: Tu as becomes t'as in nearly every spoken sentence — keeping the full form sounds formal or slow. J'ai stays j'ai; it's already short enough.

examples.french: T'as vu ça ? T'as eu de la chance.
examples.english: Did you see that? You were lucky.

explanation.body: Most French past tenses are built with avoir + past participle. Once you control j'ai, t'as, il a, on a, vous avez, ils ont, you can form the passé composé of hundreds of verbs immediately.

examples.french: On a mangé. Ils ont rien dit. T'as eu peur ?
examples.english: We ate. They said nothing. Were you scared?

nodes.street_notes: tu as → t'as (near-universal); il y a → y'a. j'ai stays j'ai.

nodes.title: aller — to go

explanation.header: The most irregular verb you need
explanation.body: Aller is fully irregular — vais, vas, va, allez, vont share nothing with the infinitive. Learn the six forms cold, because aller runs through daily French constantly: movement, greetings, near-future plans.

examples.french: On va au cinéma ce soir.
examples.english: We're going to the cinema tonight.

explanation.body: Ça va is simultaneously a question and an answer — and the default French greeting. It covers how things are going, not just physical movement.

examples.french: Ça va ? — Ça va.
examples.english: How's it going? — Fine.

examples.french: Ça va pas du tout.
examples.english: Things are not going well at all.

explanation.header: Near future: skip the real future tense
explanation.body: Aller + infinitive is how French handles upcoming events in everyday speech. The inflected future tense (j'irai) exists but sounds formal; je vais aller or on va faire is what you actually hear.

examples.french: J'vais appeler. On va voir.
examples.english: I'm going to call. We'll see.

explanation.body: Aller takes être as its auxiliary, not avoir — so the past participle agrees with the subject. English speakers forget this and write j'ai allé, which is wrong. Add -e for feminine, -s for plural.
bullets: je suis allé / je suis allée
on est allés (mixed or male group), on est allées (all female)

examples.french: T'es allé où ? — J'suis allée chez ma sœur.
examples.english: Where did you go? — I went to my sister's place.

nodes.street_notes: je vais → j'vais; ça va is the workhorse. Passé composé with être: je suis allé(e).

nodes.title: faire — to do / to make

explanation.body: Faire is the verb for doing and making — two English verbs, one French one. The forms are irregular and must be memorised, but the spoken core is small: fais, fait, and font cover nearly everything.

examples.french: Je fais la vaisselle. On fait quoi ce soir ?
examples.english: I'm doing the dishes. What are we doing tonight?

explanation.body: Tu fais quoi ? is the standard spoken question — no inversion, no est-ce que. English speakers waste time constructing formal questions; just drop quoi at the end.

examples.french: Tu fais quoi là ? — Je fais rien.
examples.english: What are you doing? — I'm not doing anything.

explanation.body: Faire + infinitive means to have something done or to make someone do it. This construction trips English speakers because French uses no extra word — faire does the whole job.

examples.french: Il fait pleurer tout le monde.
examples.english: He makes everyone cry.

examples.french: Je vais faire réparer ça.
examples.english: I'm going to get that fixed.

explanation.body: Je fais drops to j'fais. The imparfait stem stays fais- throughout, so je faisais and ils faisaient sound nearly identical — the endings are swallowed.

examples.french: J'fais comme je peux. On faisait ça tout le temps.
examples.english: I do what I can. We used to do that all the time.

nodes.street_notes: je fais → j'fais; tu fais quoi ? is the default question form.

nodes.title: vouloir — to want

explanation.body: Vouloir expresses want or desire. Follow it with an infinitive for an action, or a noun for a thing. Never insert 'de' or 'que' between vouloir and the infinitive — that's the reflex English pulls from 'want to'.

examples.french: Je veux partir. Tu veux un café ?
examples.english: I want to leave. You want a coffee?

explanation.header: The stem breaks in the middle
explanation.body: Je, tu, il, ils take the veu- stem; vous keeps the full voul-. This split is irregular but consistent — the same shape appears every time you use this tense.

examples.french: j'veux, tu veux, il veut — but vous voulez, ils veulent
examples.english: two stems, one verb

explanation.body: Je veux said straight sounds blunt in French, the way 'I want' can in English. In any request to someone unfamiliar, swap in je voudrais. The table here covers the indicative; the conditional is the next unit.

examples.french: J'veux de l'eau. → Je voudrais de l'eau.
examples.english: I want water. → I'd like some water. (softer, use this to a server)

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a specific decision or attempt: j'ai voulu means 'I tried to' or 'I decided to'. Use imparfait for an ongoing state of wanting in the background of a story.

examples.french: J'ai voulu partir, mais j'ai raté le bus.
examples.english: I tried to leave, but I missed the bus.

examples.french: Je voulais te voir mais j'avais pas le temps.
examples.english: I wanted to see you but I didn't have time.

nodes.street_notes: je veux → j'veux; tu veux → tu veux (no contraction, consonant).

nodes.title: pouvoir — to be able to / can

explanation.body: Use it to say you're able to do something or have permission to. It always precedes an infinitive — no 'à' or 'de' in between. That missing preposition is the standard English-speaker slip.

examples.french: Je peux venir demain.
examples.english: I can come tomorrow.

examples.french: On peut entrer ?
examples.english: Can we come in?

explanation.body: The stem splits: peux/peux/peut/peuvent for the singular and third-plural, pouvez for vous. The x ending on je and tu is the same oddity you see in vouloir — it replaced an older 's'. Learn the pair together.

examples.french: Tu peux répéter ? Ils peuvent pas venir.
examples.english: Can you repeat that? They can't come.

explanation.body: Je drops its vowel in fast speech — j'peux, j'pouvais. The string j'peux pas (I can't) is one of the most frequent refusals in spoken French; hear it, copy it, own it.

examples.french: J'peux pas ce soir, désolé.
examples.english: I can't tonight, sorry.

examples.french: J'pouvais pas savoir.
examples.english: I couldn't have known.

explanation.body: Use the passé composé (j'ai pu) for a specific moment when you managed — or failed — to do something. Use the imparfait (je pouvais) for ongoing ability in the past, the way things used to be.

examples.french: J'ai pu lui parler hier.
examples.english: I managed to talk to him yesterday.

examples.french: Avant, je pouvais courir des heures.
examples.english: I used to be able to run for hours.

nodes.street_notes: je peux → j'peux; 'je peux pas' → 'j'peux pas' is one of the most common street strings.

nodes.title: devoir — to have to / must

explanation.body: Devoir covers must, have to, and should — one verb where English uses three. The key split: strong obligation uses the present, softer 'should' leans on the conditional (you'll see that later), and past obligation almost always lands in the imparfait.

examples.french: Je dois partir. On devait l'appeler.
examples.english: I have to leave. We were supposed to call him.

explanation.body: English speakers miss this use: devoir + infinitive expresses logical deduction. Il doit être fatigué doesn't mean he's obligated to be tired — it means he must be tired. Context separates obligation from deduction.

examples.french: T'as dû te tromper.
examples.english: You must have made a mistake.

examples.french: Il doit être dans les embouteillages.
examples.english: He must be stuck in traffic.

explanation.header: The irregular forms to lock in
explanation.body: Doivent (ils) and dû (past participle — always with the circumflex) are the two stumbling points. The circumflex on dû exists only to distinguish it from the article du — without it, spelling would be ambiguous.

examples.french: Ils doivent bosser. J'ai dû attendre.
examples.english: They have to work. I had to wait.

explanation.body: Je dois compresses to j'dois in fast speech. For future obligation, j'vais devoir is more natural than the full future tense je devrai.

examples.french: J'dois y aller. On va devoir patienter.
examples.english: I've gotta go. We're going to have to wait.

nodes.street_notes: je dois → j'dois.

nodes.title: savoir — to know (facts, how to)

explanation.body: French splits 'to know' in two. Savoir is for facts, information, and abilities — things you can state or do. Connaître is for familiarity with a person or place. English speakers collapse both into 'know', which causes real errors.

examples.french: Je sais qu'il est parti. Je sais nager.
examples.english: I know he left. I know how to swim.

examples.french: Je connais Paris. Je connais Marie.
examples.english: I know Paris. I know Marie. (connaître, not savoir)

explanation.body: Savoir + infinitive means you have the skill — no 'comment' needed. Adding 'comment' isn't wrong but sounds bookish. The short form is what French speakers actually say.

examples.french: Elle sait conduire. Tu sais cuisiner ?
examples.english: She can drive. Do you know how to cook?

explanation.body: Je sais pas drops the ne (standard spoken French), compresses to j'sais pas, then the j' fuses with sais into chais pas — the phrase you hear constantly and the name of this course. The spelling 'chais' just captures how it sounds; it's not a separate word.

examples.french: Chais pas. → J'sais pas. → Je ne sais pas.
examples.english: I don't know. (street → casual → formal)

explanation.header: Past tense: su for sudden knowledge
explanation.body: The passé composé j'ai su means you found something out at a specific moment. For a state of knowing that was ongoing, use the imparfait je savais.

examples.french: J'ai su la vérité hier.
examples.english: I found out the truth yesterday.

examples.french: Je savais pas que c'était fermé.
examples.english: I didn't know it was closed.

nodes.street_notes: je sais pas → j'sais pas → chais pas (the app's namesake).

nodes.title: dire — to say / to tell

explanation.body: Dire is irregular in one spot only: vous dites, not vous disez. Every other irregular -re verb takes vous -ez, so this sticks out — memorise it as a single exception. The rest of the present tense is stable.

examples.french: Vous dites quoi ?
examples.english: What are you saying?

explanation.body: Dire means 'say' or 'tell'. English speakers hit trouble when 'tell' takes a person: in French, that person is an indirect object, so you need a pronoun like lui or leur, not a preposition.

examples.french: Je lui dis la vérité.
examples.english: I'm telling him the truth.

examples.french: Dis-moi ce qui s'est passé.
examples.english: Tell me what happened.

explanation.body: Where English uses 'you say', 'they say', or 'people say', French uses on dit. It's the standard way to quote common expressions or report what's generally accepted.

examples.french: On dit 'bonne chance' avant un exam.
examples.english: People say 'bonne chance' before an exam.

explanation.body: Je dis drops its vowel to j'dis in fast speech. The past j'ai dit is short and common — dit never changes form in the passé composé regardless of who's speaking.

examples.french: J'dis pas ça. T'as dit quoi ?
examples.english: I'm not saying that. What did you say?

nodes.street_notes: je dis → j'dis; on dit for general statements.

nodes.title: voir — to see

explanation.body: English splits 'see' and 'meet/visit a person', but voir handles both. Using rencontrer where voir belongs is the main English-speaker error.

examples.french: Je vois le médecin demain. On se voit ce soir ?
examples.english: I'm seeing the doctor tomorrow. Shall we meet up tonight?

explanation.body: Most forms use voi-, but vous and ils break: voyez, voient. The imparfait keeps voy- throughout — voyais, voyait, voyiez — and that's actually the regular -ais pattern applied to voy-.

examples.french: Ils voient tout. Vous voyez ce que je veux dire ?
examples.english: They see everything. You see what I mean?

explanation.header: vu is the past you'll use most
explanation.body: The passé composé j'ai vu covers one-off events. T'as vu is also a street-level filler meaning roughly 'you know?' or 'right?' — you'll hear it constantly at sentence edges.

examples.french: T'as vu le film ? — Ouais, j'ai vu ça hier, t'as vu.
examples.english: Did you see the film? — Yeah, I saw it yesterday, you know.

explanation.body: Je drops to j' before vois and voyais. Tu vois alone, mid-sentence, works exactly like 'you know' — pure filler, not a real question.

examples.french: J'vois pas ce qu'il veut, tu vois.
examples.english: I don't see what he wants, you know.

nodes.street_notes: je vois → j'vois; tu vois as a filler ('you know').

nodes.title: prendre — to take

explanation.body: Prendre means 'take', but French uses it where English reaches for other verbs: ordering food, catching transport, making a turn. When you order at a bar or buy a ticket, prendre is the verb.

examples.french: Je prends un café. On prend le métro.
examples.english: I'll have a coffee. We're taking the metro.

explanation.body: Prendre is irregular: the stem shrinks to prend- for the singular and on, then doubles the n in ils — ils prennent. That double-n changes the vowel before it, so prennent sounds like 'pren' not 'prahn'.

examples.french: il prend / ils prennent
examples.english: he takes / they take — different sounds

explanation.body: The past participle is pris — memorise it once, then it stays constant across every subject. Use it for completed grabs, trips, decisions.

examples.french: T'as pris quoi ? J'ai pris le train.
examples.english: What did you have/take? I took the train.

explanation.body: Je drops its e before prends and prenais, giving j'prends and j'prenais. This happens because spoken French avoids two consecutive vowel sounds where it can.

examples.french: J'prends ça. J'vais prendre le bus.
examples.english: I'll take that. I'm going to take the bus.

nodes.street_notes: je prends → j'prends; the café-order verb: je prends un café.

nodes.title: venir — to come

explanation.header: Être, not avoir, in the past
explanation.body: Venir takes être in the passé composé, so the past participle agrees with the subject. English speakers default to avoir — that's the mistake. If the subject is feminine, add -e; plural, add -s.

examples.french: Elle est venue hier. Ils sont venus ensemble.
examples.english: She came yesterday. They came together.

explanation.header: A stem that splits in two
explanation.body: The present tense uses two stems: vien- for je, tu, il, ils and ven- for nous and vous. That split is the only hard thing here — the endings are standard -ir endings.

examples.french: je viens / on vient / ils viennent — but vous venez
examples.english: the double-n in ils viennent shows the stem shift

explanation.body: Je viens drops to j'viens in speech. Tu viens ? said alone is an invitation — no verb de besoin, no extra words.

examples.french: T'es venu ou pas ? J'viens, attends.
examples.english: Did you come or not? I'm coming, hang on.

explanation.body: Use the passé composé for a specific trip or arrival. Use the imparfait for habitual past coming — every Sunday, as a kid. Use futur proche for plans, which is almost always how French speakers talk about near-future movement.

examples.french: Il venait tous les étés. On va venir ce soir.
examples.english: He used to come every summer. We're coming tonight.

nodes.street_notes: je viens → j'viens; tu viens ? as a one-word invitation. Passé composé with être.

nodes.title: mettre — to put / to put on

explanation.body: Mettre means to put, but it stretches further than English 'put'. You use it to put on clothes, to turn on a device, and to start something playing. English speakers miss these uses and reach for the wrong verb.
bullets: put something somewhere: mettre les clés sur la table
put on clothing: mettre un manteau
turn on / start: mettre la télé, mettre de la musique

explanation.body: The present drops to a single-t stem: met-. That makes je mets, tu mets, il met all sound identical. Only mettez and mettent restore the double-t from the infinitive.

examples.french: Tu mets quoi comme veste ?
examples.english: What jacket are you putting on?

examples.french: Ils mettent la musique fort.
examples.english: They're playing the music loud.

explanation.body: The past participle is mis — irregular, so learn it once. Past tense questions about where you left something or how long you took are where mis earns its keep.

examples.french: T'as mis combien de temps ?
examples.english: How long did you take?

examples.french: J'ai mis mon téléphone là.
examples.english: I put my phone there.

explanation.body: Je mets drops its vowel: j'mets. In fast speech you'll also hear j'mets ça là for 'I'll put that there', often replacing a whole description of action.

examples.french: J'mets le son, OK ?
examples.english: I'll put some music on, yeah?

nodes.title: parler — to speak / to talk

explanation.body: Parler is the model for every regular -er verb — about 80% of French verbs. Learn this shape and you know the default pattern for everything from écouter to travailler. Drop the -er, then add the endings.
bullets: Infinitive stem: parl-
Endings: -e, -es, -e, -e, -ez, -ent

explanation.body: Je parle, tu parles, il parle, on parle, ils parlent are five spellings of the same sound: parl. Only vous parlez sounds different. In real conversation you follow context and subject pronouns, not sound, to track who's speaking.

examples.french: Il parle vite. Ils parlent vite.
examples.english: He speaks fast. They speak fast. — identical out loud

explanation.body: Passé composé for a finished event; imparfait for ongoing habit or background. English speakers overuse the passé composé because 'I talked' feels like one clean thing — but 'we always talked' or 'she was talking' both need the imparfait.

examples.french: J'ai parlé au médecin ce matin.
examples.english: I talked to the doctor this morning.

examples.french: On parlait tout le temps de ça.
examples.english: We always talked about that.

explanation.body: J'vais parler and t'as parlé are standard spoken compression — the full forms sound formal in casual conversation.

examples.french: T'as parlé à qui ? J'vais parler à lui demain.
examples.english: Who did you talk to? I'm going to talk to him tomorrow.

nodes.street_notes: The model -er verb: one spoken form for je/tu/il/on/ils (parle/parles/parlent all sound identical).

nodes.title: aimer — to like / to love

explanation.body: Bare aimer means love for people and strong preference for things. Add bien to soften it to ordinary liking. English speakers skip bien and accidentally declare love for coffee.

examples.french: J'aime bien ce quartier. J'aime Marie.
examples.english: I like this neighbourhood. I love Marie.

explanation.body: J'aime is elision — required French, not slang. The -ent on ils aiment is silent, so j'aime and ils aiment sound identical. Context tells them apart.

examples.french: j'aime / tu aimes / il aime / ils aiment
examples.english: four spellings, two sounds: aim and aimez

explanation.body: Tu drops to t' before a vowel in fast speech, so t'aimes, t'as aimé, t'aimais are what you'll hear. Je vais aimer compresses to j'vais aimer.

examples.french: T'aimes ça ? T'as aimé le film ?
examples.english: You like that? Did you like the film?

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a finished reaction — you saw something, you liked it or you didn't. Use imparfait for an ongoing state in the past: what you used to like or what you liked throughout a period.

examples.french: J'ai aimé la soirée. J'aimais bien ce café avant.
examples.english: I liked the evening. I used to like that café.

nodes.street_notes: j'aime is elision, not slang — it's the only correct form. j'aime bien = I like (softer than j'aime).

nodes.title: acheter — to buy

explanation.body: Acheter has a silent e in the stem. When the ending adds no pronounced vowel, French props that e up with an accent grave — è — so the syllable stays audible. When the ending is already pronounced, like in vous achetez, the stem doesn't need help.
bullets: è in: j'achète, tu achètes, il achète, ils achètent
plain e in: nous achetons (not in this course), vous achetez

explanation.body: Use acheter any time money changes hands for goods. The error English speakers make is reaching for prendre — 'I'll take this' in a shop is j'achète ça, not je prends ça, unless you literally mean grabbing it.

examples.french: J'achète ce truc.
examples.english: I'm buying this thing.

examples.french: On a acheté des clopes au bureau de tabac.
examples.english: We bought cigarettes at the tabac.

explanation.body: T'achètes replaces tu achètes completely in casual speech. J'vais acheter is the default for 'I'm going to buy' — a plan, an intention, something imminent.

examples.french: T'achètes quoi ?
examples.english: What are you buying?

examples.french: J'vais acheter à manger.
examples.english: I'm going to grab some food.

explanation.body: Nearly every real purchase is a completed event, so passé composé dominates. Imparfait fits repeated habit only.

examples.french: J'ai acheté ça hier. / T'as acheté ça où ?
examples.english: I bought that yesterday. / Where'd you buy that?

examples.french: Avant, j'achetais tout en ligne.
examples.english: I used to buy everything online.

nodes.street_notes: Stem-change -er verb: è in the singular/ils forms (j'achète), plain e when the ending is pronounced (vous achetez).

nodes.title: manger — to eat

explanation.body: Manger follows the standard -er pattern with one small fix: French keeps a soft g sound throughout, so whenever an ending would harden the g, an e stays in to protect it. That's why the imparfait gives you je mangeais, not je mangais.

examples.french: je mangeais, tu mangeais, il mangeait
examples.english: the e holds the soft g in every imparfait form

explanation.body: Use the passé composé for a finished meal; use the imparfait for habits or interrupted eating. The futur proche covers any plan, near or far.

examples.french: J'ai mangé à midi. Je mangeais quand il a appelé. On va manger où ?
examples.english: I ate at noon. I was eating when he called. Where are we going to eat?

explanation.body: Rising intonation on on mange ? turns it into 'shall we eat?' — common and natural. The present tense doubles as a near-future invitation in spoken French.

examples.french: On mange ? — Ouais, j'ai faim.
examples.english: Shall we eat? — Yeah, I'm hungry.

explanation.body: T'as mangé compresses tu as; j'vais manger drops the e from je. Both are normal in casual speech — not sloppy, just fast.

examples.french: T'as mangé ce matin ? J'vais manger un truc vite fait.
examples.english: Did you eat this morning? I'm going to grab something quick.

nodes.street_notes: on mange ? as an invitation. Imparfait keeps the e: je mangeais.

nodes.title: partir — to leave

explanation.body: Partir is about the person going — it never takes an object. English 'leave' can take one ('I'm leaving the house'), but partir can't. For that, use quitter. Partir just says you're gone.

examples.french: Je pars. / Je quitte la maison.
examples.english: I'm leaving. / I'm leaving the house.

explanation.body: Most -ir verbs keep a stem like finiss-, but partir drops down to par- in the singular. The plural restores the t: ils partent. The singular has no ending sound at all — je pars, tu pars, il part are all one syllable: par.

examples.french: Il part à midi. Ils partent demain.
examples.english: He's leaving at noon. They're leaving tomorrow.

explanation.body: Partir takes être, not avoir, in the passé composé — so the past participle agrees with the subject. Feminine adds -e, mixed or masculine plural adds -s.

examples.french: Elle est partie tôt. On est partis sans toi.
examples.english: She left early. We left without you.

explanation.header: On part ? is a full sentence
explanation.body: On part ? with rising intonation is the standard spoken way to say 'shall we go?' — short, natural, used constantly. J'vais partir and on va partir cover any near-future plan.

examples.french: On part ? — Ouais, j'vais partir dans cinq minutes.
examples.english: Shall we go? — Yeah, I'm leaving in five minutes.

nodes.street_notes: je pars → j'pars; on part ? = shall we go? Passé composé with être: je suis parti(e).

nodes.title: finir — to finish

explanation.body: Finir is the model for regular -ir verbs. The key move: plural forms grow an -iss- block that singular forms don't have. That block is predictable — once you see it here, you own it for choisir, réfléchir, remplir, and dozens more.

examples.french: je finis / nous finissons — il choisit / ils choisissent
examples.english: singular: no -iss- / plural: -iss- every time

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a finished event: j'ai fini. Use imparfait for an ongoing or repeated past state: je finissais toujours tard. English speakers often default to passé composé for both — that's the mistake.

examples.french: J'ai fini le rapport hier. Je finissais toujours à minuit.
examples.english: I finished the report yesterday. I always finished at midnight.

explanation.body: C'est fini is the phrase you'll hear constantly — relationships, arguments, a film, leftover pizza. It's not conjugated for person; it just means 'it's over' or 'it's done'. Futur proche contracts in speech: j'vais finir, not je vais finir.

examples.french: C'est fini entre eux. J'vais finir ça ce soir.
examples.english: It's over between them. I'm going to finish that tonight.

nodes.street_notes: The model regular -ir verb (-iss- in plural forms). c'est fini = it's over.

nodes.title: donner — to give

explanation.body: Donner is fully regular. Learn the endings here and every other regular -er verb costs you nothing extra. The one spoken fact worth knowing: je donne, tu donnes, il donne, and ils donnent all sound identical — the written -s and -nt are silent.

examples.french: Je donne mon numéro. Ils donnent quoi ?
examples.english: I'm giving my number. What are they giving?

explanation.body: Use donner for handing something over, giving information, or producing a result. English speakers sometimes reach for offrir (to offer as a gift) when donner is right — donner covers everyday giving, offrir is for wrapped presents.

examples.french: Tu me donnes l'adresse ?
examples.english: Can you give me the address?

examples.french: Ça donne quoi, ce film ?
examples.english: What's that film like?

explanation.body: Donne-moi is the direct imperative — short, normal, not rude by itself. J'vais donner is how futur proche sounds at speed; the full je vais is there in writing but the vowel drops in real speech.

examples.french: Donne-moi deux minutes.
examples.english: Give me two minutes.

examples.french: J'vais lui donner ça demain.
examples.english: I'll give it to him tomorrow.

explanation.body: Donner takes avoir, and the past participle donné is regular. Reach for j'ai donné for completed handovers. Imparfait — je donnais — covers repeated past giving: 'I used to give', 'I was giving every week'.

examples.french: T'as donné ton avis ?
examples.english: Did you give your opinion?

examples.french: Il nous donnait toujours des conseils.
examples.english: He always used to give us advice.

nodes.street_notes: donne-moi = give me (imperative preview); ça donne quoi ? = how's it going / what's it like?

nodes.title: Politeness conditionals: je voudrais, je pourrais, il faudrait

explanation.body: Every form here is the conditional tense — French's built-in softener. The conditional adds distance between what you want and what you're demanding, which is exactly what politeness is. English does the same thing: 'I want' is abrupt; 'I would like' isn't.

examples.french: Je veux un café. → Je voudrais un café.
examples.english: I want a coffee. → I'd like a coffee.

explanation.body: Voudrais and aimerais are near-synonyms at a counter or in a restaurant; either works. Pourrais asks for permission or makes a suggestion — it's 'could I' not 'would I like'. Faudrait states what needs to happen without pointing at anyone.
bullets: voudrais / aimerais — ordering, requesting something for yourself
pourrais / pourriez — asking permission, offering a suggestion
faudrait — 'we'd need to', 'one would have to'
ce serait / ça serait — evaluating an option: 'that would be...'

examples.french: Tu pourrais répéter ? Faudrait réserver.
examples.english: Could you repeat that? We'd need to book.

explanation.body: Il faudrait loses its subject in speech — faudrait alone is standard in conversation. Ce serait shifts to ça serait because ça is the default spoken pronoun for 'it/that'. Neither is sloppy; both are expected.

examples.french: Faudrait partir. Ça serait bien.
examples.english: We'd need to leave. That would be nice.

explanation.body: Don't translate 'can I' as je peux — in a polite request je pourrais is the natural choice, just as 'could I' is softer than 'can I'. Using je veux in a shop or restaurant reads as rude; voudrais costs nothing to swap in.

examples.french: Je pourrais avoir l'addition ? J'aimerais voir la carte.
examples.english: Could I get the bill? I'd like to see the menu.

nodes.title: croire — to believe / to think

explanation.body: Croire means both 'to believe' and 'to think/reckon' — it carries opinion as much as belief. Use it where English says 'I think so', 'I don't think so', or 'Do you think...?' Don't reach for penser every time; croire is what people actually say.

examples.french: J'crois que c'est possible.
examples.english: I think it's possible.

examples.french: T'y crois vraiment ?
examples.english: Do you really believe that?

explanation.header: The stem shift: croi- vs croy-
explanation.body: All irregular -re verbs in this family swap their stem before endings that need a vowel. Croi- handles je/tu/il/ils; croy- appears whenever the ending starts with a vowel — croyez, croyais, croyaient. That single spelling rule covers the whole table.
bullets: croi-: je crois, tu crois, il croit, ils croient
croy-: vous croyez, je croyais, il croyait, ils croyaient

explanation.body: J'crois pas is spoken French for 'I don't think so' — it's a filler-level reaction, not a full sentence. Pair it with que for a clause: j'crois pas que c'est grave.

examples.french: J'crois pas. / J'crois que oui.
examples.english: I don't think so. / I think so.

explanation.header: Past tense: which one and why
explanation.body: The passé composé j'ai cru covers a finished moment of believing. The imparfait je croyais describes a background state — something you believed for a stretch of time. Because opinions are usually ongoing states, croyais is the more natural past in storytelling.

examples.french: Je croyais que c'était vrai.
examples.english: I thought it was true. (ongoing belief)

examples.french: J'ai cru ce qu'il m'a dit.
examples.english: I believed what he told me. (single moment)

nodes.street_notes: j'crois is one of the most common sentence-starters in spoken French — j'crois pas, j'crois que oui. tu crois → t'crois in fast speech.

nodes.title: penser — to think

explanation.body: penser que introduces a thought or opinion — j'pense que c'est bon. penser à means to have someone or something on your mind. Mixing the two is the main English-speaker slip.

examples.french: J'pense que c'est possible.
examples.english: I think it's possible.

examples.french: Je pense à toi.
examples.english: I'm thinking about you.

explanation.body: In real speech j'pense que is as automatic as 'I think' in English. The ne in je ne pense pas disappears entirely — you'll only ever hear j'pense pas.

examples.french: J'pense que c'est chaud.
examples.english: I think that's rough.

examples.french: J'pense pas que c'est une bonne solution.
examples.english: I don't think it's a good solution.

explanation.body: All six present forms share the stem pens-. Four of them — je, tu, il, ils — sound identical: pense. Only pensez and pensons (→ on pense) differ audibly, so context carries the meaning.

examples.french: Il pense quoi ? On pense pareil.
examples.english: What does he think? We think the same.

explanation.body: The participle is pensé — drop -er, add -é. Futur proche is j'vais penser, imparfait is je pensais. None of these have irregularities.

examples.french: T'as pensé à ça ? J'pensais que t'étais là.
examples.english: Did you think about that? I thought you were there.

nodes.street_notes: j'pense is the everyday opinion opener — j'pense que c'est bon. penser à quelqu'un = think about; penser que = think that.

drills.english: I think it's not serious.
drills.french_formal: Je pense que ce n'est pas grave.

drills.english: Do you think it's possible?

drills.english: He thinks we can eat here.

drills.french_street: Il pense qu'on peut manger ici.

nodes.title: passer — to pass / to stop by

explanation.body: Passer uses être in the passé composé when it means moving through or stopping by with no object — je suis passé. Switch to avoir the moment it takes a direct object — j'ai passé une bonne soirée. The object is the trigger.

examples.french: Il est passé ce matin.
examples.english: He stopped by this morning.

examples.french: On a passé une heure là-bas.
examples.english: We spent an hour there.

explanation.body: Passer covers passing by, dropping in, and spending time — three distinct English verbs. Context decides which reading. Passer also slots into fixed chunks you'll hear constantly.
bullets: j'passe te voir = I'll drop in on you
ça passe = it's acceptable / it fits
ça va passer = it'll pass (pain, a phase)

explanation.body: Je passe compresses to j'passe; je suis passé to j'suis passé. The rest follows the standard ne-drop and t'es rules you already have.

examples.french: J'passe demain, ça te va ?
examples.english: I'll stop by tomorrow, does that work for you?

nodes.street_notes: je suis passé = I stopped by (être when moving); j'ai passé une bonne soirée (avoir when it takes an object). j'passe te voir = I'll drop in on you. ça passe = it's acceptable / it fits.

nodes.title: arriver — to arrive / to manage / to happen
nodes.english: to arrive / to manage / to happen

explanation.body: Arriver means to arrive, but also to happen (ça arrive) and to manage to do something (arriver à + infinitive). The third meaning catches English speakers off guard — j'arrive pas à dormir has nothing to do with travel.

examples.french: J'arrive pas à ouvrir ça.
examples.english: I can't manage to open this.

examples.french: Ça arrive.
examples.english: It happens. / No worries.

explanation.header: Être, not avoir, in the past
explanation.body: Like aller and partir, arriver uses être in the passé composé. That means the past participle agrees with the subject — arrivée for a woman, arrivés for a group.

examples.french: Elle est arrivée en retard. On est arrivés.
examples.english: She arrived late. We arrived.

explanation.header: The one form you'll say constantly
explanation.body: J'arrive ! means 'coming!' — you'll say it every time someone calls you. On the street, j'arrive pas à is the everyday replacement for je ne peux pas when something just won't work.

examples.french: J'arrive ! — J'arrive pas à me lever.
examples.english: Coming! — I can't manage to get up.

nodes.street_notes: j'arrive ! = coming! — said constantly. ça arrive = it happens / no big deal. j'arrive pas à... = I can't manage to... (the everyday 'I can't').

drills.english: I'm coming!

nodes.title: rester — to stay

explanation.header: The false friend that bites everyone
explanation.body: Rester means to stay, not to rest. English 'rest' is se reposer. Translating 'I'm staying home' as je me repose is the first mistake; the second is reaching for rester when you mean to rest.

examples.french: Je reste à la maison.
examples.english: I'm staying home. (not resting)

examples.french: Je dois me reposer.
examples.english: I need to rest.

explanation.body: In the third person, rester also means 'there is/are ... left'. This impersonal use is everywhere in daily speech and has nothing to do with staying anywhere.

examples.french: Il reste du pain ? Il en reste deux.
examples.english: Is there any bread left? There are two left.

explanation.body: Rester takes être as its auxiliary, so the past participle agrees with the subject. This is the same group as partir, arriver, and aller — verbs of position change or, here, position held.

examples.french: Elle est restée. On est restés.
examples.english: She stayed. We stayed.

explanation.body: Je compresses before a consonant: j'reste, j'restais, j'vais rester. Tu es resté becomes t'es resté in real speech.

examples.french: J'reste là. T'es resté combien de temps ?
examples.english: I'm staying here. How long did you stay?

nodes.street_notes: false friend: rester = stay, never 'rest'. j'reste là = I'm staying here. il reste... = there's ... left (il en reste ? = is there any left?).

nodes.title: aider — to help

explanation.body: English helps people; French helps them too, directly. Aider takes its object with no preposition. Adding 'à' before a person is the reflex error English speakers make. Reserve 'aider à + infinitive' only when saying what task you're helping with.

examples.french: Tu peux m'aider ?
examples.english: Can you help me?

examples.french: Je l'aide à trouver une solution.
examples.english: I'm helping him find a solution.

explanation.body: Drop -er, add the endings. In speech, je/tu/il/on all sound identical: aide. You distinguish them by pronoun alone. The -ent in ils aident is silent.

examples.french: j'aide, t'aides, il aide, on aide — same sound
examples.english: four spellings, one pronunciation

explanation.body: Tu aides contracts to t'aides. The imperative aide ! is the quickest way to ask for help; pair it with moi for emphasis. For polite requests, tu peux m'aider ? is what you actually hear — not inversion.

examples.french: Aide-moi ! / T'as aidé quelqu'un ?
examples.english: Help me! / Did you help someone?

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a completed act of helping. Use imparfait for ongoing or repeated help in the past — a job, a habit, a background state.

examples.french: Il m'a aidé hier. Il m'aidait tout le temps.
examples.english: He helped me yesterday. He was always helping me.

nodes.street_notes: j'aide by formal elision. tu aides → t'aides. Direct object, no preposition: tu peux m'aider ? = can you help me?

drills.english: Can you help me?

nodes.title: regarder — to watch / to look at

explanation.body: Regarder takes its object directly, no 'at' required. English 'look at' and 'watch' both map to regarder. Adding 'à' after regarder is the main English-speaker error.

examples.french: Je regarde la télé. Il regarde le ciel.
examples.english: I'm watching TV. He's looking at the sky.

explanation.body: Je, tu, il, and on all sound identical — regarde. The -ent of ils regardent is also silent. You only hear a new ending at vous regardez.

examples.french: j'regarde, tu regardes, il regarde, on regarde — même son.
examples.english: four spellings, one sound

explanation.body: As a standalone command between friends, regarde ! means 'look!' or 'check this out.' It works as a filler-level attention-getter, the way English speakers say 'look—' before making a point.

examples.french: Regarde, c'est pas possible.
examples.english: Look, that's not on.

explanation.body: Use j'ai regardé for a completed viewing — you watched a film, a match, an episode. Use je regardais for the background state — what was on, what you were in the middle of watching when something else happened.

examples.french: J'ai regardé le film. Je regardais la télé quand il est arrivé.
examples.english: I watched the film. I was watching TV when he arrived.

nodes.street_notes: no 'at': regarder quelque chose directly — j'regarde la télé. regarde ! alone = look! — constant between friends.

drills.english: I'm watching TV.

nodes.title: trouver — to find

explanation.body: Trouver means to find a thing and to find (think) something is a certain way. The opinion use trips English speakers: 'I find it interesting' is j'trouve ça intéressant, not a form of penser or croire. Both meanings run through the same verb.

examples.french: J'trouve pas mes clés.
examples.english: I can't find my keys.

examples.french: Tu trouves ça normal ?
examples.english: You think that's normal?

explanation.header: One form does most of the work
explanation.body: Trouve covers je, il, and on in speech — three subjects, one pronunciation. The -ent ending of ils trouvent is silent, so ils trouvent and il trouve sound identical. Context tells you which.

examples.french: On trouve, il trouve, ils trouvent — same sound.
examples.english: three subjects, one spoken form

explanation.body: J'trouve que is your second opinion frame after j'crois que. J'trouve pas covers both 'I don't think so' and 'I can't find it'. Tu trouves ? alone means 'you think so?' — a complete sentence in French conversation.

examples.french: J'trouve que c'est chaud. — Tu trouves ?
examples.english: I think it's rough. — You think so?

explanation.body: The passé composé is j'ai trouvé — fully regular, no surprises. Reach for the imparfait when setting a scene: je trouvais ça bien describes an ongoing past opinion. Futur proche je vais trouver works for plans and reassurances.

examples.french: J'ai trouvé. On va trouver une solution.
examples.english: I found it. We'll find a solution.

nodes.street_notes: j'trouve que... = I find/think that... — the second opinion frame after j'crois. tu trouves ? = you think so? j'trouve pas = I don't think so / can't find it.

drills.french_formal: Je trouve que ce n'est pas possible.

drills.english: They're going to find that interesting.

nodes.title: attendre — to wait (for)

explanation.body: Attendre is your first regular -re verb; entendre and perdre follow the same shape exactly. Drop the -re, then add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. In speech that means one sound — attend — covers je, tu, il, and on.

examples.french: j'attends, t'attends, il attend, on attend
examples.english: four spellings, one sound

explanation.body: English waits FOR things; French waits them directly. Attendre takes its object with no preposition, and inserting a 'pour' is the classic English-speaker error.

examples.french: J'attends le bus.
examples.english: I'm waiting for the bus.

examples.french: Tu attends quelqu'un ?
examples.english: Are you waiting for someone?

explanation.body: Attends ! by itself is the universal 'hang on' — filler-level frequent between friends. The past is regular too: j'ai attendu.

examples.french: Attends, j'arrive.
examples.english: Hang on, I'm coming.

nodes.street_notes: j'attends is already elided in formal French; tu attends → t'attends. Attends ! alone is the universal 'hang on'. attendre takes its object with no preposition — j'attends le bus, never 'pour'.

drills.english: I'm waiting for the metro.

nodes.title: appeler — to call

explanation.body: Appeler doubles the l when the ending is silent — j'appelle, il appelle, ils appellent — and keeps one l when the ending is pronounced: vous appelez. The spelling tracks stress, not randomness: the double l forces the open [ɛ] sound you need before a silent ending.

examples.french: j'appelle, tu appelles, il appelle / vous appelez
examples.english: double l (silent ending) / single l (ending pronounced)

explanation.body: Je t'appelle means 'I'll call you' just as often as 'I'm calling you.' English speakers over-reach for the futur proche here — in real speech, the present handles it.

examples.french: Je t'appelle ce soir.
examples.english: I'll call you tonight.

explanation.body: Tu appelles compresses to t'appelles. J'appelle stays two syllables but the je is often swallowed in fast speech. The passé composé is fully regular: j'ai appelé, t'as appelé.

examples.french: T'appelles comment ? J'ai appelé, t'as pas répondu.
examples.english: What's your name? I called, you didn't answer.

nodes.street_notes: double-l where the ending is silent (j'appelle), single l where it's pronounced (vous appelez). j'appelle = I'll call — présent does the near future. je t'appelle = I'll call you.

drills.english: I'll call you tonight.

nodes.title: connaître — to know (people, places)

explanation.body: Connaître means knowing through familiarity — people, places, things you have direct experience of. Savoir means knowing a fact or knowing how to do something. English uses 'know' for both, which is why French learners constantly mix them up.

examples.french: Je connais Paris. Je sais qu'il fait froid.
examples.english: I know Paris (I've been there). I know it's cold.

examples.french: Tu connais Marie ? Tu sais où elle habite ?
examples.english: Do you know Marie? Do you know where she lives?

explanation.body: The stem is connais- throughout the present, but il/elle gets a circumflex: connaît. That accent is the only written irregularity in the present. Every other tense is built on connaiss- and stays consistent.

examples.french: je connais / il connaît / ils connaissent
examples.english: the three written shapes of the present

explanation.body: J'connais is the spoken norm. The negative j'connais pas does the work of 'I don't know' for people and places — chais pas covers facts. Use j'connais pas when someone asks if you know a person, a bar, a neighbourhood.

examples.french: J'connais pas ce resto.
examples.english: I don't know that restaurant.

examples.french: T'as connu quelqu'un là-bas ?
examples.english: Did you meet anyone there?

explanation.body: Because connaître describes a state of familiarity, it sits naturally in the imparfait — ongoing background knowledge. Use the passé composé only when the meeting or discovery is a single completed event.

examples.french: Je connaissais bien ce quartier.
examples.english: I knew that neighbourhood well. (ongoing state)

examples.french: J'ai connu Marie à Paris.
examples.english: I met Marie in Paris. (the moment it happened)

nodes.street_notes: connaître = know people/places/things by acquaintance; savoir = know facts/how-to. j'connais pas = don't know it/them. tu connais ? = you know it? — the recommendation opener.

drills.english: I know that café.
Do you know this neighbourhood?
He doesn't know Paris.
We know a good restaurant.
I don't know it — is it good?
drills.french_formal: Je ne le connais pas — c'est bien ?

drills.english: They know each other well.
I met him in Paris.
Did you meet someone at the café?
drills.french_formal: Tu as connu quelqu'un au café ?

drills.english: We didn't know each other before.
drills.french_formal: On ne s'est pas connus avant.

drills.english: They met on the métro.
drills.french_formal: Ils se sont connus dans le métro.
drills.french_street: Ils se sont connus dans le métro.

drills.english: I used to know this street well.
He knew everyone at the café.

drills.french_street: Il connaissait tout le monde au café.

nodes.title: porter — to wear / to carry

explanation.body: Porter covers both 'to wear' and 'to carry' — French doesn't split them. Context does the work: a vêtement (garment) makes it wear, a sac makes it carry. English speakers often reach for mettre (to put on) when describing what someone is wearing right now — that's natural but different; porter means they have it on.

examples.french: Il porte un manteau noir.
examples.english: He's wearing a black coat.

examples.french: Tu portes le sac ?
examples.english: Are you carrying the bag?

explanation.body: Porter is fully regular -er. In speech, je/tu/il/on all sound identical — port. Only vous portez and ils portent sound different, and ils portent only if a vowel follows (liaison). That one spoken stem is your anchor.

examples.french: j'porte, tu portes, il porte, on porte — same sound
examples.english: four spellings, one spoken form

explanation.header: ça se porte — the phrase to own
explanation.body: The reflexive ça se porte means 'people wear that' or 'that's in style' — you'll hear it whenever someone defends a fashion choice. It's a ready-made sentence that also shows you how French uses se porter impersonally.

examples.french: Le beige, ça se porte beaucoup.
examples.english: Beige is worn a lot these days.

explanation.body: Je drops to j' before a consonant: j'porte, j'portais, j'vais porter. Tu + vowel contracts as always: t'as porté. Ne disappears: j'porte jamais de cravate.

examples.french: J'porte jamais de cravate.
examples.english: I never wear a tie.

examples.french: T'as porté quoi hier ?
examples.english: What did you wear yesterday?

nodes.street_notes: wear and carry are the same verb — context decides. j'porte jamais de noir = I never wear black. ça se porte = people wear that.

nodes.title: sortir — to go out / to leave

explanation.body: Both mean 'to leave' but partition the job differently. Sortir means going out of an enclosed space — a room, a building, your house, your routine. Partir means leaving a place or person behind. When English says 'go out tonight', French reaches for sortir every time.

examples.french: Je sors du bureau à six heures.
examples.english: I leave the office at six.

examples.french: On sort ce soir ?
examples.english: We going out tonight?

explanation.body: Drop the -tir and its stem consonant for je/tu (je sors, tu sors), keep the t for il/on/ils (il sort, ils sortent). The endings are identical to partir — if you know one, you know the other.

examples.french: Il sort. Ils sortent. On sort.
examples.english: He's leaving. They go out. We're heading out.

explanation.header: It takes être in the past
explanation.body: Like all movement verbs in this family, sortir builds its passé composé with être, not avoir. The participle is sorti, and it agrees with the subject in writing.

examples.french: J'suis sorti hier soir. On est sortis ensemble.
examples.english: I went out last night. We went out together.

explanation.body: This fixed phrase means 'to date someone' — you'll hear it constantly. Context always makes it clear whether it means 'going out with' in the physical or romantic sense.

examples.french: T'es sorti avec elle longtemps ?
examples.english: Did you date her for long?

nodes.street_notes: same shape as partir: sors/sort split. on sort ce soir ? = we going out tonight? je suis sorti hier = I went out last night. sortir avec quelqu'un = to date someone.

nodes.title: comprendre — to understand

explanation.body: Comprendre is prendre with a prefix, so the forms are identical. The irregular stem compren- shortens to comprenn- in ils comprennent — double n, one extra syllable. That's the only surprise.

examples.french: je comprends, il comprend, ils comprennent
examples.english: I understand, he understands, they understand

explanation.body: English often adds 'it' or 'that': 'I don't understand it.' French drops the object unless it's specific. J'comprends pas alone is the survival phrase — lean on it.

examples.french: J'comprends pas. Je comprends la situation.
examples.english: I don't understand. I understand the situation.

explanation.body: The past participle compris doubles as a spoken check: compris ? means 'got it?' and c'est compris means 'understood.' Both are higher-frequency than any full sentence with comprendre.

examples.french: C'est compris ? — Ouais, compris.
examples.english: Got it? — Yeah, understood.

explanation.body: Present for the live moment, imparfait for ongoing past states ('I wasn't understanding anything'), passé composé for the click of sudden understanding, futur proche for reassurance.

examples.french: J'comprenais rien. Là j'ai compris. Ça va aller, tu vas comprendre.
examples.english: I wasn't getting anything. Now I got it. It'll be fine, you'll understand.

nodes.street_notes: prendre with a prefix — same forms exactly. j'comprends pas = the survival phrase. compris ? / c'est compris = got it?

nodes.title: entendre — to hear

explanation.body: Entendre is a regular -re verb. Drop the -re, add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. The stem entend covers je, tu, il, and on in speech — one syllable, four spellings.

examples.french: j'entends, t'entends, il entend, on entend
examples.english: four spellings, one spoken stem

explanation.body: Entendre is passive — sound reaches you. Écouter is active — you direct your attention. English 'listen' is écouter; English 'hear' is entendre. Mixing them is the main mistake.

examples.french: J'entends la musique mais j'écoute pas.
examples.english: I can hear the music but I'm not listening.

explanation.body: Like attendre, entendre takes its object directly — no preposition between verb and noun. The past participle is entendu; the imparfait stem is entend-.

examples.french: T'as entendu ça ?
examples.english: Did you hear that?

examples.french: J'entendais rien.
examples.english: I couldn't hear anything.

explanation.header: The street phrase to know now
explanation.body: J'entends rien is the go-to for 'I can't hear anything' — on calls, in loud places, everywhere. Rien alone drops the ne; no other change needed.

examples.french: J'entends rien, tu peux parler plus fort ?
examples.english: I can't hear anything, can you speak louder?

nodes.street_notes: attendre's twin — same -re shape. j'entends rien = I can't hear anything. entendre = hear (passive), écouter = listen (active).

drills.english: I can't hear anything.

nodes.title: demander — to ask (for)

explanation.body: Demander is a false friend. It means to ask or to ask for, never to demand. English speakers either avoid it or reach for it when they mean 'command'. Use it any time you'd say 'ask' in English.

examples.french: Je demande l'addition.
examples.english: I'm asking for the bill. (no 'pour')

examples.french: Il a demandé de l'aide.
examples.english: He asked for help.

explanation.body: Like attendre, demander takes its object directly — no 'pour'. To name the person you're asking, add 'à': demander à quelqu'un. Both facts in one sentence: je demande à Paul l'addition.

examples.french: J'lui demande l'heure.
examples.english: I'm asking him the time.

explanation.body: The reflexive je me demande is its own idiom — it has nothing to do with asking someone. It's high-frequency in casual speech and worth locking in as a chunk.

examples.french: J'me demande si c'est possible.
examples.english: I wonder if that's possible.

explanation.body: Demander is fully regular -er. On the street: je demande → j'demande, je demandais → j'demandais, je vais demander → j'vais demander. No surprises.

examples.french: J'demande, t'as demandé, il va demander.
examples.english: I ask, you asked, he's going to ask.

nodes.street_notes: false friend: demander = ask, not demand. No 'for': j'demande l'addition. demander à quelqu'un = ask someone. j'me demande = I wonder.

drills.french_formal: Je me demande si c'est possible.

nodes.title: chercher — to look for

explanation.body: Chercher already means 'to look for'. Adding 'pour' is the English-speaker reflex — resist it. The object follows directly, the same way attendre takes its object without a preposition.

examples.french: Je cherche la gare.
examples.english: I'm looking for the station.

examples.french: Tu cherches quoi ?
examples.english: What are you looking for?

explanation.body: Je, tu, il, and on all collapse to the same spoken syllable: cherch. Only vous (cherchez) and ils (cherchent — same sound as the singular) differ on paper. In conversation, context and pronouns do all the disambiguation work.

examples.french: J'cherche, tu cherches, il cherche, on cherche — even: ils cherchent.
examples.english: Five spellings, one sound.

explanation.body: Je cherche… is the natural way to ask for directions or find a shop assistant. It's polite, functional, and more natural than a question in many situations.

examples.french: Je cherche la rue de Rivoli.
examples.english: I'm looking for Rue de Rivoli.

examples.french: On cherchait un restau, du coup on a demandé.
examples.english: We were looking for a restaurant, so we asked.

nodes.street_notes: no 'for' — the searching is built in: j'cherche la gare = I'm looking for the station. The shop/directions opener: je cherche...

nodes.title: laisser — to leave / to let

explanation.body: Laisser does both 'leave something behind' and 'let someone do something'. English splits these between two verbs; French doesn't. The pattern for 'let' is laisser + person + infinitive — no extra word needed.

examples.french: Je laisse mon sac ici.
examples.english: I'm leaving my bag here.

examples.french: Laisse-moi parler.
examples.english: Let me speak.

explanation.body: Laisse tomber is the street phrase for 'forget it / drop it'. It works on its own or in a sentence. You'll hear it constantly; internalize it as a chunk, not a translation.

examples.french: Laisse tomber, c'est pas grave.
examples.english: Forget it, it's not a big deal.

explanation.body: Je laisse becomes j'laisse in fast speech. Tu as laissé contracts to t'as laissé. Ne drops everywhere: 'tu laisses pas' is the spoken norm.

examples.french: J'laisse un pourboire. T'as laissé les clés ?
examples.english: I'm leaving a tip. Did you leave the keys?

explanation.body: Laisser follows the standard -er pattern exactly. The participle laissé is predictable. If you know parler and manger, you already know this conjugation.

examples.french: Il laissait toujours un message. On va laisser ça.
examples.english: He always left a message. We're going to leave that.

nodes.street_notes: laisse tomber = forget it / drop it — essential street chunk. laisse-moi... = let me... j'laisse un pourboire = I'm leaving a tip.

nodes.title: écouter — to listen (to)

explanation.body: Écouter takes its object directly. English says 'listen TO the radio'; French just says j'écoute la radio. Slipping in a pour or à is the automatic English-speaker mistake.

examples.french: J'écoute la radio. Il écoute sa copine.
examples.english: I'm listening to the radio. He's listening to his girlfriend.

explanation.body: At the start of a sentence, écoute (or écoutez) means 'look' or 'listen' — a way to signal you're about to say something important. It's discourse glue, not literally about hearing.

examples.french: Écoute, c'est pas possible.
examples.english: Look, that's not possible.

examples.french: Écoutez, c'est important.
examples.english: Listen, this is important.

explanation.body: Good news: écouter is fully regular -er, so every rule you learn here works for hundreds of other verbs. The only street adjustments are ne-drop and the t'écoutes contraction for tu.

examples.french: T'écoutes quoi ? On écoutait la radio.
examples.english: What are you listening to? We were listening to the radio.

nodes.street_notes: no 'to': j'écoute la radio. écoute... at the start of a sentence = look,... / listen,... — the French discourse opener.

nodes.title: perdre — to lose

explanation.body: Perdre is the third regular -re verb. Drop -re, add the same endings: -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. That means je perds, tu perds, il perd — three spellings, one sound in speech.

examples.french: j'perds, tu perds, il perd, on perd
examples.english: four forms, one spoken sound

explanation.body: J'ai perdu uses avoir — it's an action (you lost something). Je suis perdu uses être — it's a state (you are lost right now). English uses 'I'm lost' for both; French splits them. C'est perdu means the thing is gone for good.

examples.french: J'ai perdu mon téléphone.
examples.english: I lost my phone. (action, avoir)

examples.french: Je suis perdu. C'est perdu.
examples.english: I'm lost. / It's gone.

explanation.body: It covers losing objects, losing time, losing a game, and losing your way. English sometimes reaches for 'miss' or 'waste' — French keeps perdre: perdre du temps is wasting time.

examples.french: On perd du temps. Il perd toujours ses clés.
examples.english: We're wasting time. He always loses his keys.

explanation.body: Je drops the schwa before a consonant: j'perds, j'perdais, j'vais perdre. T'as perdu is the natural accusation between friends.

examples.french: T'as perdu quoi ?
examples.english: What did you lose?

nodes.street_notes: third of the -re family. j'ai perdu mon téléphone — the traveler's sentence. je suis perdu = I'm lost (état, with être). c'est perdu = it's gone.

drills.english: I'm losing my phone.

drills.french_formal: Il perd ses clés à chaque fois.
drills.french_street: Il perd ses clés à chaque fois.

drills.french_street: Vous perdez la tête — c'est pas normal !

nodes.title: tenir — to hold

explanation.body: Tenir means to hold, but it reaches further: tenir à something means to care about it, and the imperative tiens ! means 'here you go' or 'oh, look at that' — you'll hear it constantly in handovers and reactions. Don't reach for 'avoir' when the idea is holding or caring about.

examples.french: Je tiens la porte. Tiens, c'est pour toi.
examples.english: I'm holding the door. Here you go, it's for you.

examples.french: J'tiens à ce plan.
examples.english: I care about this plan.

explanation.body: Tenir and venir share identical endings — the only difference is the auxiliary: tenir takes avoir in the passé composé, venir takes être. The stem doubles in the ils form: ils tiennent.
bullets: je/tu/il: tiens / tiens / tient
ils: tiennent (two n's, stems doubles)
past participle: tenu (avec avoir)

explanation.body: Je tiens drops to j'tiens in flow; tu as tenu becomes t'as tenu. The double-n in ils tiennent is already the spoken norm — no further reduction.

examples.french: J'tiens le café, t'as tenu la porte ?
examples.english: I'm holding the coffee, did you hold the door?

nodes.street_notes: venir's shapes exactly, with avoir in the past. tiens ! = here you go / oh look — constant in handovers and surprise. tenir à = to care about.

nodes.title: vivre — to live

explanation.header: Vivre vs habiter — pick the right one
explanation.body: Both mean 'to live', but they're not interchangeable. Habiter is purely about location — your address. Vivre adds the sense of actually experiencing a place or a life. Use vivre when location would sound thin.

examples.french: J'vis à Paris. / J'habite à Paris.
examples.english: Both work for stating where you live.

examples.french: On vit bien ici.
examples.english: Life is good here. (habiter would sound wrong)

explanation.header: An irregular stem you can't skip
explanation.body: The infinitive is vivre but the present stem drops to vi-: je vis, tu vis, il vit. That -s/-s/-t pattern follows the regular -re shape, so the irregularity is only the stem. Vivent keeps the full stem.

examples.french: Il vit seul. Ils vivent ensemble.
examples.english: He lives alone. They live together.

explanation.header: Vécu: for chapters of a life
explanation.body: The past participle vécu is irregular — memorise it once. Use it for a defined period you lived somewhere or a phase that's over. English says 'lived' for both states and events; French uses vécu for those closed life chapters.

examples.french: J'ai vécu deux ans en France.
examples.english: I lived in France for two years.

examples.french: T'as vécu quoi comme expériences ?
examples.english: What experiences have you lived through?

nodes.street_notes: j'vis à Paris = I live in Paris — self-introduction core (habiter works too). j'ai vécu deux ans en France = I lived there two years — vécu for life chapters.

nodes.title: essayer — to try

explanation.header: Essayer needs DE before an infinitive
explanation.body: Unlike vouloir or pouvoir, essayer links to a following verb with de. Drop the de and the sentence breaks. This is the one structural trap for English speakers.

examples.french: J'essaie de parler français.
examples.english: I'm trying to speak French.

examples.french: Il essaie de payer.
examples.english: He's trying to pay.

explanation.body: When the ending is silent — j'essaie, tu essaies, il essaie, ils essaient — the y becomes i. Where the ending is pronounced, y stays: vous essayez, j'essayais. The rule is about sound, not person.

examples.french: j'essaie / vous essayez / ils essaient
examples.english: I try / you try / they try

explanation.header: Futur proche does the heavy lifting
explanation.body: In real speech, j'vais essayer is how you say 'I'll try' — both a promise and a soft commitment. The futur simple exists but you'll hear this form in almost every conversation.

examples.french: J'vais essayer, chais pas si ça marche.
examples.english: I'll try, not sure if it'll work.

explanation.body: Use the imparfait when you tried something repeatedly or habitually in the past — not once, but over time. For a single finished attempt, use the passé composé.

examples.french: J'essayais de le joindre mais c'était pas possible.
examples.english: I kept trying to reach him but it wasn't possible.

examples.french: J'ai essayé une fois.
examples.english: I tried once.

nodes.street_notes: y → i where the ending is silent (j'essaie), y kept where pronounced (vous essayez). essayer DE + infinitive: j'essaie d'apprendre. j'vais essayer = I'll try.

nodes.title: travailler — to work

explanation.body: Travailler follows the regular -er pattern exactly — strip -er, add the endings. In speech, je/tu/il/on all sound like 'travay', and the silent -ent of ils is the only trap for reading aloud.

examples.french: j'travaille, t'travailles, il travaille, on travaille
examples.english: four spellings, one spoken sound

explanation.body: The -aille ending looks unusual but is just the -er pattern on a stem ending in -ill-. Pronounce it like 'trav-eye'. No special rule is firing here.

examples.french: Je travaille demain.
examples.english: I'm working tomorrow.

explanation.header: When you'll actually use each tense
explanation.body: Present covers today's schedule and general habit. Futur proche handles any upcoming plan. Passé composé nails a finished stint; imparfait describes what working was like as background.

examples.french: T'as travaillé hier ? — Ouais, j'travaillais jusqu'à vingt heures.
examples.english: Did you work yesterday? — Yeah, I was working until eight.

explanation.body: Bosser and taffer mean the same thing and are everywhere. Recognise them, but produce travailler — it lands in every register and you won't have to switch.

examples.french: Tu travailles où ? — J'travaille au centre.
examples.english: Where do you work? — I work in the centre.

nodes.street_notes: the small-talk verb: tu travailles où ? / j'travaille demain. Street synonyms you'll hear: bosser, taffer — recognize them, say travailler.

nodes.title: rentrer — to go home / to get back
nodes.english: to go home / to get back

explanation.body: Rentrer doesn't just mean 'to return' — it means to go back to your base, usually home. When you say je rentre, the destination is already inside the verb. Adding 'à la maison' is possible but almost never said.

examples.french: Je rentre. C'est l'heure.
examples.english: I'm heading home. It's time.

examples.french: Tu rentres quand ?
examples.english: When are you getting back?

explanation.header: Être, not avoir, in the past
explanation.body: Like all movement-to-a-place verbs, rentrer takes être in the passé composé. That means the past participle agrees with the subject — rentré for one man, rentrés for a group.

examples.french: Je suis rentré tard. On est rentrés ensemble.
examples.english: I got home late. We got home together.

explanation.body: Je suis rentré compresses to j'suis rentré in speech. Tu es rentré becomes t'es rentré — the same contraction you already use for all être passé composé. Present j'rentre drops the e of je before the consonant r.

examples.french: J'rentre. T'es rentré à quelle heure ?
examples.english: I'm heading home. What time did you get back?

explanation.body: Use the imparfait when rentrer is background — the ongoing state ('I was on my way home when…'). For a single completed trip, use the passé composé.

examples.french: Je rentrais quand ça a sonné.
examples.english: I was heading home when it rang.

nodes.street_notes: 'home' is built in: j'rentre = I'm heading home. tu rentres quand ? = when are you getting back? je suis rentré tard = I got home late (être).

nodes.title: commencer — to start

explanation.body: Commencer is regular -er except the ç: the cedilla appears only before a, to keep the soft-s sound. So je commençais but je commence — the a in the imparfait ending triggers it. Everywhere else, plain c.

examples.french: je commence / je commençais
examples.english: I start / I was starting — one letter changes, nothing else

explanation.body: When commencer links to another verb, it needs à. English uses 'to' or '-ing'; French always uses à here. Drop it and the sentence is broken.

examples.french: Ça commence à m'énerver.
examples.english: It's starting to annoy me.

examples.french: On commence à comprendre.
examples.english: We're starting to understand.

explanation.header: What gets said on the street
explanation.body: Je contracts before consonants: j'commence, j'commençais, j'vais commencer. Ça commence quand? is the natural word order for 'when does it start?' — inversion is formal and rare here.

examples.french: Ça commence quand ?
examples.english: When does it start?

examples.french: J'commence à avoir faim.
examples.english: I'm starting to get hungry.

nodes.street_notes: commencer À + infinitive: ça commence à m'énerver = it's starting to annoy me. ça commence quand ? = when does it start? (The ç spelling only shows up before a: je commençais.)

drills.english: It starts at eight.
I'm starting to understand.

When does it start?

Did you start already?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu as déjà commencé ?

nodes.title: oublier — to forget

explanation.body: Oublier follows the standard -er pattern exactly — drop the -r for the stem, add the usual endings. The only thing to notice is the double-i in vous oubliiez (imparfait): the stem already ends in -i, then the ending adds another. It looks strange but follows the rule mechanically.

examples.french: j'oublie, on oublie, ils oublient
examples.english: present — three spellings, two sounds

examples.french: vous oubliiez
examples.english: imparfait — two i's, one sound

explanation.body: Oublier is transitive: it always takes what you forgot. English speakers sometimes say 'I forgot about it' — French drops the 'about' and uses the direct object pronoun. The passé composé is its most natural home; forgetting is almost always a one-off event, not a background state.

examples.french: J'ai oublié mon téléphone.
examples.english: I forgot my phone.

examples.french: J'ai oublié — no 'de' or 'à' needed after it.
examples.english: just verb + object

explanation.body: These are chunk-level frequent — learn them as units.
bullets: j'oublie tout le temps — I always forget (self-deprecating, very common)
t'as oublié ? — did you forget? (gentle accusation between friends)
oublie ! — forget it (sharper, more dismissive than laisse tomber)

nodes.street_notes: j'ai oublié — the daily confession. j'oublie tout le temps = I always forget. oublie ! = forget it (sharper than laisse tomber).

nodes.title: payer — to pay (for)

explanation.body: Payer takes its object directly. English pays FOR things; French doesn't add a preposition. Inserting 'pour' after payer is the reflex to override.

examples.french: Je paie le café.
examples.english: I'm paying for the coffee.

examples.french: T'as payé l'addition ?
examples.english: Did you pay for the bill?

explanation.header: The spelling shift in present tense
explanation.body: The y → i swap in je/tu/il/ils (je paie, ils paient) exists because French avoids the sound 'yuh' before a silent ending. On and vous keep the y: on paie sounds identical to il paie, but vous payez keeps the y because the ending is pronounced.

examples.french: je paie, tu paies, il paie — but — vous payez
examples.english: y becomes i in the silent-ending persons only

explanation.body: Two fixed street chunks are far more frequent than a bare conjugation: c'est moi qui paie (it's on me) and on paie comment ? (how are we paying?). The participle payé is completely regular — no surprises.

examples.french: C'est moi qui paie. On paie comment ?
examples.english: It's on me. How are we paying?

examples.french: J'vais payer par carte.

nodes.title: boire — to drink

explanation.body: Boire has two stems: boi- for singular and ils, buv- for vous and the imparfait. The jump is jarring but the pattern is fixed — once you see it, every tense follows it.
bullets: boi-: je bois, tu bois, il boit, ils boivent
buv-: vous buvez, je buvais, il buvait, ils buvaient

explanation.body: The participle bu is short and irregular — nothing to build it from, just memorise it. It pairs with avoir: j'ai bu, t'as bu, on a bu.

examples.french: T'as bu quoi ? On a bu un verre.
examples.english: What did you drink? We had a drink.

explanation.body: Boire means any drinking — coffee, water, alcohol. 'Boire un verre' is the set phrase for having a drink socially. 'Tu bois quoi ?' is the standard bar opener; learn it as a chunk.

examples.french: Tu bois quoi ? J'bois un café.
examples.english: What are you drinking? I'm having a coffee.

explanation.body: Je bois → j'bois, je buvais → j'buvais, je vais boire → j'vais boire. The boi- stem already starts with a consonant, so the vowel of je always drops in fast speech.

examples.french: J'bois rien le matin.
examples.english: I don't drink anything in the morning.

present.label: Présent
present.note: French has one present where English has three. Je mange covers 'I eat', 'I'm eating', and 'I do eat' — there is no -ing form to build, so the présent works harder than its English cousin. It also reaches into the future whenever a time word does the pointing.

contrasts.point: One form carries both English presents — context does the rest.
With a concrete time, the présent covers the near future on its own.

passe_compose.label: Passé composé
passe_compose.note: The tense for events: things that happened, finished, and moved the story forward. It answers 'what happened?'. Built from avoir or être plus the participle — which is why the contraction lands on the auxiliary (t'as mangé), not the verb.

contrasts.b_english: I was eating when you called.
contrasts.point: Event vs background: the passé composé is the thing that happened; the imparfait is what was already going on.
A bounded event takes passé composé; an unbounded backdrop takes imparfait.
Same verb, different question: outcome as an event vs description of how it was.

imparfait.label: Imparfait
imparfait.note: The tense for background: how things were, what used to happen, what was mid-flight when the event hit. If the passé composé is the photo, the imparfait is the scenery it was taken in. English gives you two reliable signals: 'was …-ing' and 'used to'.

contrasts.point: Mid-action vs completed: the imparfait leaves the film running.

contrasts.a_french: On allait au café tous les matins.
contrasts.a_english: We used to go to the café every morning.
contrasts.b_french: On est allés au café ce matin.
contrasts.b_english: We went to the café this morning.
contrasts.point: Habit in the past is imparfait territory; the one-time trip is an event.
Descriptions and states live in the imparfait; happenings live in the passé composé.

futur_proche.label: Futur proche
futur_proche.note: Aller + infinitive is how spoken French handles the future — plans, predictions, things about to happen. It is the English 'going to', built the same way, and in conversation it covers nearly everything the written future tense does. If you can conjugate aller, you can say anything in the future.

contrasts.point: Same meaning; the futur proche is the spoken norm, the futur simple reads as written or formal register.
The everyday reassurance phrases are all futur proche — fixed frames worth owning whole.
